items.title: Why the Friedewald formula fails at high triglycerides and in dysbetalipoproteinemia
items.content:
LDL-C is usually calculated, not measured: LDL-C = total cholesterol − (TG/5) − HDL-C, where TG/5 estimates VLDL cholesterol (the normal ~1:5 cholesterol:TG ratio of VLDL). This breaks down in two key situations:
• TG >400 mg/dL — the TG/5 estimate of VLDL cholesterol becomes unreliable, so the formula cannot be used (directly measure LDL-C).
• Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia (FDBL) — the VLDL particles are TG-depleted and cholesterol-ENRICHED, so the 1:5 assumption is wrong and the formula is invalid.

Non-HDL-C (= total cholesterol − HDL-C) avoids this problem and captures the cholesterol in VLDL/IDL, which is also atherogenic.
keyPoints: Friedewald invalid if TG >400 or in FDBL (cholesterol-rich VLDL).
Non-HDL-C = TC − HDL-C captures all atherogenic (apoB) cholesterol.
Directly measure LDL-C when the formula can't be used.
items.reference: Screening: "This formula is reasonably accurate if test results are obtained on fasting plasma and if the TG level does not exceed ~200 mg/dL; by convention, it cannot be used if the TG level is >400 mg/dL."

items.title: Why apoB and Lp(a) add to LDL-C in risk assessment
items.content:
ApoB and Lp(a) refine cardiovascular risk beyond LDL-C:
• Each atherogenic particle (chylomicron remnant, VLDL, IDL, LDL) carries exactly ONE apoB molecule, so plasma apoB counts the number of atherogenic particles — a better risk marker than LDL-C (or even non-HDL-C), especially when LDL particles are small and dense (e.g., FCHL), where apoB is disproportionately high relative to LDL-C.
• Lp(a) is an LDL-like particle with apo(a); it is a CAUSAL, highly heritable, independent risk factor for ASCVD and aortic stenosis. An elevated Lp(a) warrants more aggressive LDL-C lowering.
keyPoints: ApoB counts atherogenic particles (one apoB per particle).
ApoB > LDL-C as a risk marker, especially with small dense LDL.
Lp(a) is causal/heritable for ASCVD + aortic stenosis → lower LDL-C more aggressively.
items.reference: Screening: "measurement of plasma apoB levels may provide a better assessment of cardiovascular risk than the LDL-C level... Lp(a) is now established as causal factor for ASCVD and aortic stenosis, and an elevated level of Lp(a) serves as an independent risk factor."

items.title: Why severe hypertriglyceridemia threatens the pancreas, not the arteries
items.content:
Severe HTG (fasting TG >500 mg/dL) is dominated by chylomicrons/VLDL from impaired lipolysis, and the principal danger is ACUTE PANCREATITIS — so the treatment goal is to reduce TG to <500 to prevent it. By contrast, premature ASCVD is generally NOT a feature of pure chylomicronemia (e.g., familial chylomicronemia syndrome), because the giant TG-rich particles are too large to enter the arterial wall.

Key nuance: it is MODERATE HTG (150–500 mg/dL) — with its associated small dense LDL, low HDL, and remnant particles — that raises ASCVD risk. So management of severe HTG targets pancreatitis; management of moderate HTG targets ASCVD (LDL-C/non-HDL-C/apoB).
keyPoints: Severe HTG (>500) → pancreatitis risk → lower TG below 500.
Pure chylomicronemia is not strongly atherogenic.
Moderate HTG (150–500) drives ASCVD risk.
items.reference: Severe HTG: "It is medically important because it is associated with risk of acute pancreatitis... Premature ASCVD is not generally a feature of FCS"; Treatment: "moderate HTG (TG 150–500 mg/dL) is associated with increased ASCVD risk."

items.title: How to recognize familial chylomicronemia syndrome
items.content:
FCS is recessive impaired lipolysis from biallelic loss of LPL (most common) or its partners APOC2, APOA5, GPIHBP1, or LMF1. Clues:
• Fasting TG persistently >1000 mg/dL with a creamy supernatant on standing plasma.
• Eruptive xanthomas (yellow-white papules on back/buttocks/extensors), lipemia retinalis (opalescent retinal vessels), hepatosplenomegaly.
• Recurrent acute pancreatitis (may present in childhood).
• A total cholesterol-to-TG ratio <... (very TG-predominant); genetic FCS panel can confirm but is not required.
Notably, premature ASCVD is NOT typical. Treatment is extreme dietary fat restriction (~15 g/day) with fat-soluble vitamins; fibrates/fish oil are usually ineffective. APOC3 (volanesorsen) and ANGPTL3 silencing are emerging; in APOC2 deficiency, fresh-frozen plasma supplies apoC-II during acute pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Recessive LPL-pathway failure → TG often >1000, creamy plasma.
Eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, pancreatitis; ASCVD not typical.
Treat with severe fat restriction; fibrates/fish oil usually don't work.
items.reference: FCS: "Genetic deficiency or inactivity of LPL results in impaired lipolysis and profound elevations in plasma TGs... Dietary fat intake should be markedly restricted (to as little as 15 g/d)... fish oils or fibrates... are unlikely to be effective."

items.title: Why most severe hypertriglyceridemia is multifactorial, not monogenic
items.content:
Apart from FCS and familial partial lipodystrophy, there are no classic Mendelian causes of HTG. Most severe HTG (~1 in 1000) is POLYGENIC: a combination of (1) rare heterozygous variants in the five FCS genes (which only cause disease when homozygous) and (2) a high burden of common TG-raising variants — unmasked by secondary factors.

The quantitatively dominant secondary factors are obesity, type 2 diabetes, insulin resistance, high-carbohydrate diet, and alcohol. This is why management starts by identifying and correcting secondary factors; pharmacologic TG-lowering is added mainly to prevent pancreatitis when TG stays >500 despite lifestyle change.
keyPoints: Most HTG = polygenic risk + secondary factors (not a single gene).
Dominant drivers: obesity, T2DM, insulin resistance, high-carb diet, alcohol.
Correct secondary factors first; drugs mainly to prevent pancreatitis.
items.reference: Multifactorial HTG: "the quantitative most important factors promoting HTG include obesity, type 2 diabetes, insulin resistance, high-carbohydrate diet, and alcohol use... management of patients with this condition is mostly focused on reduction of TGs to prevent pancreatitis."

items.title: How familial hypercholesterolemia raises LDL-C
items.content:
FH is autosomal DOMINANT and converges on reduced LDL-receptor clearance of LDL. Three genes:
• LDLR (most common) — fewer/defective receptors.
• APOB (familial defective apoB) — mutated LDL-receptor-binding domain of apoB-100, so LDL binds poorly.
• PCSK9 GAIN-of-function — excess PCSK9 degrades more LDL receptors (ADH type 3).
(Conversely, PCSK9 LOSS-of-function and truncating APOB mutations LOWER LDL-C.)

Heterozygous FH (~1 in 250) gives LDL-C usually >190 from birth, with normal TG; untreated men have ~50% chance of MI before age 60. Look for corneal arcus and tendon xanthomas (Achilles, dorsum of hands). Because it's dominant, family-based cascade screening is highly effective.
keyPoints: FH = dominant ↓LDL-receptor clearance (LDLR, APOB, gain-of-function PCSK9).
Het FH ~1/250: LDL >190 from birth, tendon xanthomas, ~50% MI in men by 60.
Dominant → cascade-screen first-degree relatives.
items.reference: FH: "FH is caused by mutations that lead to reduced function of the LDL receptor... mutations in at least two other genes, APOB and PCSK9, can also cause FH... untreated men with heterozygous FH have an ~50% chance of having a myocardial infarction before age 60 years."

items.title: Homozygous FH: why receptor-negative patients don't respond to statins
items.content:
HoFH (both LDLR alleles, or two FH genes) causes LDL-C ~400 to >1000 with devastating childhood/early-adult ASCVD — characteristically aortic-root atherosclerosis causing supravalvular/aortic stenosis and coronary ostial stenosis.

Crucial distinction: statins and PCSK9 inhibitors work by upregulating the LDL receptor, so RECEPTOR-NEGATIVE patients (no functional receptor) get NO response, whereas RECEPTOR-DEFECTIVE patients respond modestly. This is why HoFH needs receptor-INDEPENDENT therapies: lomitapide (MTP inhibitor) and mipomersen (apoB antisense) reduce hepatic VLDL/LDL production, and evinacumab (anti-ANGPTL3) lowers LDL independent of the receptor; LDL apheresis and liver transplant are options.
keyPoints: HoFH: LDL 400–>1000, aortic-root/ostial atherosclerosis in childhood.
Receptor-negative → no statin/PCSK9i response (they need a working receptor).
Use receptor-independent therapy: lomitapide, mipomersen, evinacumab, apheresis.
items.reference: HoFH: "Although receptor-negative patients have no response to statins and PCSK9 inhibitors, receptor-defective patients can have modest responses... a small-molecule inhibitor of MTP and an antisense oligonucleotide to apoB, and an antibody that inhibits ANGPTL3 are approved for the treatment of patients with HoFH."

items.title: Why familial dysbetalipoproteinemia needs a 'second hit' and has unique xanthomas
items.content:
FDBL (type III hyperlipoproteinemia) is recessive: apoE2/E2 homozygosity gives apoE with low LDL-receptor affinity, so remnant particles (chylomicron remnants, IDL) accumulate. But only a minority of E2/E2 homozygotes (population ~0.5%) develop overt FDBL (~1 in 10,000) — a precipitating 'second hit' is needed (high-fat diet, obesity, diabetes, hypothyroidism, menopause, alcohol).

Diagnostic hallmarks: cholesterol and TG elevated to a SIMILAR degree with NORMAL HDL; pathognomonic palmar (xanthoma striata palmaris) and tuberoeruptive xanthomas; prominent peripheral vascular disease. Friedewald is invalid (cholesterol-rich VLDL); diagnose by VLDL-C:total-TG ratio >0.30 or apoE2/E2 genotyping. Diet-responsive; statins first-line.
keyPoints: apoE2/E2 + a precipitating factor → remnant accumulation.
Cholesterol ≈ TG, normal HDL; palmar + tuberoeruptive xanthomas (pathognomonic).
Diagnose by VLDL-C:TG >0.30 or apoE2/E2; treat with diet + statin.
items.reference: FDBL: "The apoE2 allele... is the cause of FDBL when present on both alleles... Two distinctive types of xanthomas, tuberoeruptive and palmar, are seen in FDBL patients... β-quantification by ultracentrifugation (ratio of directly measured VLDL cholesterol to total plasma TG >0.30)."

items.title: Familial combined hyperlipidemia: one defect, switching phenotypes
items.content:
FCHL is the most common familial lipid disorder (~1 in 100–200) and accounts for ~20% of CHD before age 60. The shared metabolic defect is hepatic OVERPRODUCTION of VLDL and apoB. Affected family members (and the same person over time) show one of three phenotypes — high LDL-C, high TG, or both — depending on diet, weight, and insulin sensitivity.

The diagnostic key is apoB: it is substantially elevated, often DISPROPORTIONATE to the LDL-C, signaling many small dense LDL particles. So a patient with mixed dyslipidemia and a high apoB relative to LDL-C, plus a family history of dyslipidemia/premature CHD, has FCHL — and merits aggressive treatment (high-intensity statin ± ezetimibe/PCSK9i/bempedoic acid).
keyPoints: Most common familial lipid disorder; overproduction of VLDL/apoB.
Phenotype switches (↑LDL, ↑TG, or both); small dense LDL.
Measure apoB — disproportionately high apoB relative to LDL-C is the clue.
items.reference: FCHL: "Patients with FCHL have substantially elevated plasma levels of apoB, often disproportionately high relative to the plasma LDL-C concentration, indicating the presence of small dense LDL particles... ~20% of patients who develop CHD under age 60 have FCHL."

items.title: Why hypothyroidism must be excluded in every patient with high LDL-C
items.content:
Thyroid hormone normally UPregulates the hepatic LDL receptor, so hypothyroidism DOWNregulates it and raises LDL-C. Because hypothyroidism is common and often subtle, EVERY patient with elevated LDL-C — especially an unexplained rise — should have a TSH measured before being labeled with a primary lipid disorder.

Thyroid replacement usually normalizes LDL-C; if it does not, a primary lipoprotein disorder is likely and a statin may be needed. Other secondary LDL-raisers: diet high in saturated/trans fats, nephrotic syndrome, cholestasis (produces Lp-X), Cushing's syndrome, and immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, sirolimus)/corticosteroids.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism downregulates the LDL receptor → high LDL-C.
Check TSH in all patients with elevated/unexplained-rising LDL-C.
Other secondary causes: nephrotic syndrome, cholestasis (Lp-X), Cushing's, cyclosporine/sirolimus.
items.reference: Secondary Factors: "Hypothyroidism is the most important medical condition causing elevated LDL-C levels... all patients presenting with elevated plasma levels of LDL-C, especially if there has been an unexplained increase in LDL-C, should be screened for hypothyroidism by measuring TSH."

items.title: How statins work and the 'rule of 6'
items.content:
Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, lowering hepatic cholesterol synthesis; the cell compensates by UPregulating the LDL receptor, accelerating LDL clearance and lowering LDL-C ~30–55%. A clinically useful nuance: once a patient is on a statin, DOUBLING the dose yields only ~6% further LDL-C reduction ('rule of 6') — so combination therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9i, bempedoic acid) is usually more effective than repeated dose-doubling.

Safety: myalgia in 3–5% (rarely myopathy/rhabdomyolysis with ↑CK); routine CK monitoring is NOT needed (asymptomatic CK elevation doesn't predict myopathy); transaminases may rise mildly/transiently; there is a small excess of incident type 2 diabetes that is outweighed by cardiovascular benefit. Watch interactions (gemfibrozil, macrolides, azole antifungals).
keyPoints: Inhibit synthesis → upregulate LDL receptor → ↓LDL-C 30–55%.
Doubling the dose adds only ~6% ('rule of 6') — combine instead.
Myalgia 3–5%; don't monitor CK routinely; small ↑diabetes outweighed by benefit.
items.reference: Statins: "the doubling of the statin dose produces a ~6% further reduction in the level of plasma LDL-C... Serum CK levels need not be monitored on a routine basis... a slight excess in incident type 2 diabetes... the cardiovascular benefits... far outweigh the slight increase in incident diabetes."

items.title: How PCSK9-targeted therapies lower LDL-C
items.content:
PCSK9 binds the LDL receptor and sends it to the lysosome for degradation, reducing receptor recycling. Antagonizing PCSK9 therefore preserves LDL receptors and clears more LDL. Two modalities:
• Monoclonal antibodies (alirocumab, evolocumab) — sequester circulating PCSK9; ~60% LDL-C reduction, modest Lp(a) lowering, reduce cardiovascular events; SC every 2 weeks.
• Inclisiran — a small interfering RNA (siRNA) silencing PCSK9 mRNA in the liver; ~60% LDL-C reduction with SC dosing every 6 MONTHS (outcomes trials ongoing).

They are second-line (added to statin) or third-line (statin + ezetimibe) in FH/ASCVD when LDL-C remains too high. The human genetics of PCSK9 loss-of-function (low LDL, protection from CHD) validated this target.
keyPoints: PCSK9 degrades LDL receptors; blocking it raises receptor numbers.
Antibodies: ~60% LDL drop, SC q2 weeks; inclisiran (siRNA) SC q6 months.
Used when statin ± ezetimibe doesn't reach goal in FH/ASCVD.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "Antibodies to PCSK9 sequester it and functionally increase the number of LDL receptors... an ~60% reduction in LDL-C... inclisiran, is a therapeutic siRNA molecule... administered subcutaneously every 6 months."

items.title: Why bempedoic acid causes less myalgia than statins
items.content:
Bempedoic acid inhibits ATP citrate lyase, an enzyme UPSTREAM of HMG-CoA reductase in cholesterol synthesis, so it also upregulates the hepatic LDL receptor — but by a different step than statins. The key clinical advantage: it is a PRODRUG activated by an enzyme (ACSVL1) that is NOT expressed in skeletal muscle, so it does not generate the active drug in muscle and is associated with much less myalgia — making it useful in statin-intolerant patients.

It lowers LDL-C ~18% added to a statin (~23% as monotherapy; ~36% combined with ezetimibe) and reduced major cardiovascular events in statin-intolerant patients. Watch for increased uric acid/gout and liver enzymes; unlike statins, it does NOT increase diabetes. Avoid with simvastatin >20 mg.
keyPoints: Inhibits ATP citrate lyase (upstream of statin target) → upregulates LDL receptor.
Prodrug not activated in muscle → less myalgia; good for statin-intolerant patients.
↑uric acid/gout; no diabetes signal; reduces MACE in statin-intolerant patients.
items.reference: Bempedoic acid: "it is a prodrug that requires activation by very-long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase-1 (ASCVL1), which is not expressed in skeletal muscle, potentially explaining why it has less association with myalgias than statins... is associated with increased uric acid levels and gout."

items.title: How ezetimibe complements statins
items.content:
Cholesterol in the gut lumen (about one-third dietary, two-thirds biliary) is absorbed by the enterocyte protein NPC1L1. Ezetimibe binds and inhibits NPC1L1, blocking ~60% of cholesterol absorption; reduced sterol delivery to the liver upregulates the LDL receptor, lowering LDL-C ~18%.

Because this mechanism is independent of (and complementary to) statins' synthesis inhibition, ezetimibe's effect is additive on a statin and has been shown to reduce major cardiovascular events when added to a statin (IMPROVE-IT principle). It is the usual second-line add-on for further LDL-C lowering; monotherapy roles are statin intolerance and sitosterolemia.
keyPoints: Blocks NPC1L1-mediated cholesterol absorption (~60%); ~18% LDL drop.
Additive to statins and reduces cardiovascular events when added.
Monotherapy mainly for statin intolerance or sitosterolemia.
items.reference: Ezetimibe: "Ezetimibe is a cholesterol absorption inhibitor that binds directly to and inhibits NPC1L1... The mean reduction in plasma LDL-C on ezetimibe (10 mg) is 18%... has been shown to significantly reduce major cardiovascular events compared with statin alone."

items.title: Fibrates, fish oils, and icosapent ethyl for triglycerides
items.content:
For severe HTG persisting >500 mg/dL despite lifestyle change, the two main classes are:
• FIBRATES (PPARα agonists): stimulate LPL, reduce apoC-III, promote fatty-acid β-oxidation → lower TG ~30%. They don't lower (and may raise) LDL-C; watch myopathy (especially with statins — avoid gemfibrozil combos), gallstones, raised creatinine, and warfarin potentiation.
• OMEGA-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) 3–4 g/day: lower TG ~30% but can raise LDL-C in some patients.

A distinct advance: icosapent ethyl, a purified EPA-only product, REDUCED cardiovascular events in patients with HTG (a benefit not shown for mixed EPA/DHA preparations). Volanesorsen (APOC3 antisense) is approved in Europe for FCS but causes severe thrombocytopenia.
keyPoints: Fibrates (PPARα): ↑LPL, ↓apoC-III → ~30% TG drop; myopathy with statins, gallstones.
Fish oils 3–4 g: ~30% TG drop (can raise LDL-C).
Icosapent ethyl (EPA-only) reduces CV events; volanesorsen (APOC3 ASO) for FCS.
items.reference: TG therapy: "Fibrates stimulate LPL activity... reduce apoC-III synthesis... reduce TG levels by ~30%... Icosapent ethyl is an EPA-only product that has been shown to reduce cardiovascular events in patients with HTG."

items.title: Why low HDL-C predicts risk but is not a treatment target
items.content:
Low HDL-C (<40 mg/dL men, <50 women) is a strong INDEPENDENT predictor of ASCVD and is built into risk calculators — but it is now considered NOT directly causal. HDL-C integrates other risk factors (it falls with high TG, insulin resistance, obesity, and inflammation), which likely explains its inverse association with disease.

The clinical consequence: HDL-C remains a useful risk biomarker, but raising HDL-C is NOT a therapeutic goal (drugs that raise HDL have not reduced events). Manage the underlying drivers (TG, weight, insulin resistance) and focus therapy on lowering apoB/LDL-C. Genetic very-low-HDL states (Tangier, LCAT deficiency, apoA-I mutations) often, surprisingly, do NOT show clearly increased ASCVD.
keyPoints: Low HDL-C is a risk marker, not a causal target.
Don't treat to raise HDL; treat the drivers (TG, weight, insulin resistance).
Genetic low-HDL syndromes often lack clearly increased ASCVD risk.
items.reference: Low HDL: "it is now considered doubtful that low HDL-C is directly causal for the development of ASCVD... it is no longer considered a target for therapeutic intervention to raise HDL-C levels in order to reduce cardiovascular risk."

items.title: Tangier disease (ABCA1 deficiency): the orange tonsils clue
items.content:
ABCA1 effluxes cellular cholesterol/phospholipid onto apoA-I to build HDL. With biallelic ABCA1 loss (Tangier disease), nascent poorly-lipidated apoA-I is rapidly cleared, so HDL-C and apoA-I are extremely low (<5 mg/dL). Cholesterol accumulates in the reticuloendothelial system, producing the distinctive findings: enlarged grayish-yellow/ORANGE tonsils, hepatosplenomegaly, and intermittent peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex).

Despite the very low HDL, ASCVD risk is less than expected — partly because LDL-C is also low. Recognizing the orange tonsils + very low HDL + neuropathy triad points to the diagnosis.
keyPoints: ABCA1 loss → can't lipidate apoA-I → HDL/apoA-I <5 mg/dL.
Orange tonsils, hepatosplenomegaly, peripheral neuropathy.
ASCVD risk lower than expected (LDL-C is also low).
items.reference: Tangier Disease: "patients with Tangier disease... have extremely low circulating plasma levels of HDL-C (<5 mg/dL)... resulting in hepatosplenomegaly and pathognomonic enlarged, grayish yellow or orange tonsils."

items.title: Why sitosterolemia doesn't respond to statins (and what does)
items.content:
Sitosterolemia is recessive loss of ABCG5/ABCG8, the transporters that normally pump plant sterols (and excess cholesterol) back into the gut lumen and bile. Without them, plant sterols and cholesterol are hyper-absorbed and under-excreted, raising hepatic sterols, which suppresses the LDL receptor → severe hypercholesterolemia with tendon xanthomas and premature ASCVD.

Two nuances make it recognizable and treatable: (1) distinctive hematology — hemolysis, splenomegaly, and megathrombocytes (plant sterols in cell membranes); (2) because the problem is ABSORPTION (not synthesis), it responds poorly to statins but WELL to diet, bile acid sequestrants, and especially cholesterol-absorption inhibitors (ezetimibe). Suspect it in severe hypercholesterolemia without family history or with statin non-response.
keyPoints: ABCG5/ABCG8 loss → plant sterol/cholesterol hyperabsorption.
Distinctive: hemolysis, splenomegaly, megathrombocytes.
Poor statin response; treat with ezetimibe/bile acid sequestrants/diet.
items.reference: Sitosterolemia: "Episodes of hemolysis and splenomegaly are a distinctive clinical feature... diet, bile acid sequestrants, and cholesterol-absorption inhibitors are the most effective agents to reduce LDL-C and plasma plant sterol levels."

items.title: Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency: a treatable cause of high LDL + fatty liver
items.content:
LALD (cholesteryl ester storage disease) is recessive LIPA loss; lysosomal acid lipase normally hydrolyzes TGs/cholesteryl esters delivered to the lysosome (e.g., via the LDL receptor). Its deficiency causes neutral-lipid accumulation in hepatocytes → progressive fatty liver/fibrosis, with elevated LDL-C, LOW HDL-C, and variable TG (impaired LDL-receptor-mediated clearance raises LDL).

The key clinical message: SUSPECT it in a NON-obese patient with high LDL-C, low HDL-C, and fatty liver WITHOUT overt insulin resistance — because it is treatable. Diagnose by a dried-blood-spot LAL activity assay (confirm with LIPA genotyping). Enzyme replacement with sebelipase alfa is highly effective; the infantile form (Wolman's disease) is rapidly fatal.
keyPoints: LIPA loss → hepatic lipid accumulation; ↑LDL, ↓HDL, fatty liver.
Suspect in non-obese patient with this pattern and no insulin resistance.
Dried-blood-spot LAL assay; treat with sebelipase alfa (enzyme replacement).
items.reference: LALD: "LALD should be suspected in nonobese patients with elevated LDL-C, low HDL-C, and evidence of fatty liver in the absence of overt insulin resistance... enzyme replacement therapy with sebelipase alfa is now available and is highly effective."

items.title: Why abetalipoproteinemia causes neurologic and retinal disease
items.content:
Abetalipoproteinemia is recessive loss of MTP (MTTP), which is required to assemble apoB-containing particles, so chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL, and apoB are UNDETECTABLE and cholesterol/TG are extremely low. Because fat-soluble vitamins depend on these particles for transport, the clinical picture is driven by VITAMIN deficiency — especially vitamin E (transported by chylomicrons to the liver and by VLDL out of it).

This produces fat malabsorption (childhood diarrhea/failure to thrive), spinocerebellar degeneration (areflexia, ataxia, spastic gait — can be misdiagnosed as Friedreich's ataxia), and pigmented retinopathy (night-blindness → vision loss). Treatment is a low-fat diet with high-dose vitamin E started as early as possible. (Parents are normal — distinguishing recessive abetalipoproteinemia from co-dominant homozygous FHBL, where parents have low LDL-C.)
keyPoints: MTP loss → no apoB particles → undetectable LDL/apoB, fat malabsorption.
Vitamin E deficiency → spinocerebellar degeneration + pigmented retinopathy.
High-dose vitamin E early; parents are normal (vs co-dominant FHBL).
items.reference: Abetalipoproteinemia: "patients with abetalipoproteinemia are markedly deficient in vitamin E... Treatment consists of a low-fat, high-caloric, vitamin-enriched diet accompanied by large supplemental doses of vitamin E."

items.title: How human 'experiments of nature' drove modern lipid drugs
items.content:
Several blockbuster therapies came directly from rare genetic LOW-lipid states:
• PCSK9 loss-of-function (low LDL, ~30–40% reduction, protection from CHD) → PCSK9 antibodies and inclisiran.
• Truncating APOB mutations (familial hypobetalipoproteinemia, low LDL) → mipomersen (apoB antisense) for HoFH.
• MTP loss (abetalipoproteinemia, absent apoB) → lomitapide (MTP inhibitor) for HoFH.
• ANGPTL3 loss (familial combined hypolipidemia: low TG, LDL, and HDL; reduced CHD) → evinacumab (anti-ANGPTL3) and ANGPTL3 silencing.
• APOC3 variants (low TG, low ASCVD) → volanesorsen (APOC3 antisense) for FCS.

The lesson: lifelong genetically low LDL/TG confers cardiovascular protection, validating these as drug targets.
keyPoints: PCSK9 LOF → PCSK9 antibodies/inclisiran.
APOB truncation → mipomersen; MTP loss → lomitapide; ANGPTL3 loss → evinacumab.
APOC3 loss → volanesorsen — lifelong low lipids = CV protection.
items.reference: Low-lipid disorders: "The discovery of this condition led to the development of therapies that antagonize or silence PCSK9... antagonize or silence ANGPTL3 to reduce LDL-C and TG levels... an antisense oligonucleotide to treat HoFH."

items.title: How to decide on a statin in primary prevention
items.content:
In established ASCVD, lower LDL-C with drug therapy as long as it remains >70 mg/dL (combination therapy if needed). In FH, treat early (ideally childhood) because of high lifetime risk regardless of calculated 10-year risk.

Otherwise, for patients >40 without ASCVD, use the ASCVD pooled-cohort calculator: a 10-year risk >7.5% supports statin therapy regardless of LDL-C level. When the decision is borderline, 'risk enhancers' help: apoB, Lp(a), and hs-CRP, and especially a CORONARY ARTERY CALCIUM (CAC) score (in men >40, women >50) — an elevated CAC justifies a statin, while a CAC of zero may support deferral.
keyPoints: ASCVD: lower LDL-C while >70; FH: treat early regardless of 10-yr risk.
Primary prevention >40: 10-yr ASCVD risk >7.5% → consider statin.
Borderline cases: use apoB, Lp(a), hs-CRP, and coronary artery calcium score.
items.reference: Pharmacologic Therapy: "a 10-year risk >7.5% merits consideration of statin therapy regardless of plasma LDL-C level... apoB, Lp(a), and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP)... a coronary artery calcium (CAC) score has been shown to provide independent information."

items.title: Why bile acid sequestrants are avoided in hypertriglyceridemia
items.content:
Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine, colestipol, colesevelam) bind intestinal bile acids and force their excretion; to refill the bile-acid pool the liver diverts cholesterol into bile-acid synthesis, depleting hepatocyte cholesterol and UPregulating the LDL receptor → lower LDL-C.

The catch: this also drives hepatic VLDL output, so they can RAISE triglycerides — meaning they should generally NOT be used in patients with hypertriglyceridemia. Their advantage is safety: not systemically absorbed (useful in pregnancy/children), with side effects limited to the GI tract (bloating, constipation). Colesevelam is also approved to lower glucose in type 2 diabetes.
keyPoints: Bind bile acids → liver makes more from cholesterol → upregulates LDL receptor.
Can RAISE triglycerides → avoid in hypertriglyceridemia.
Not absorbed → safe (pregnancy/children); GI side effects.
items.reference: Bile Acid Sequestrants: "BAS... primarily reduce plasma LDL-C levels but can cause an increase in plasma TGs. Therefore, patients with HTG generally should not be treated with bile acid-binding resins."

items.title: Why cholestasis causes hypercholesterolemia and Lp-X
items.content:
Cholesterol is normally excreted via bile; cholestasis blocks this route. Retained hepatocellular cholesterol downregulates the LDL receptor, raising LDL-C. In severe cholestasis, excess free cholesterol and phospholipid are shed into plasma as an abnormal lamellar particle, Lp-X — which lacks apoB and has an aqueous (not neutral-lipid) core, and is rich in free cholesterol.

Lp-X can deposit in the skin to produce xanthomas. Recognizing a cholestatic cause matters because the dyslipidemia reflects the liver disease rather than a primary lipid disorder. (Other liver effects: viral hepatitis can raise TG; liver failure lowers cholesterol and TG.)
keyPoints: Cholestasis blocks biliary cholesterol excretion → high LDL-C.
Severe cholestasis sheds Lp-X (apoB-free, free-cholesterol-rich) → skin xanthomas.
Liver failure lowers lipids; viral hepatitis can raise TG.
items.reference: Liver Disorders: "Cholestasis is almost invariably associated with hypercholesterolemia due to elevated LDL-C levels and, if severe, particles called Lp-X... rich in free cholesterol, and can deposit in the skin, producing xanthomas."

items.title: Familial partial lipodystrophy: an under-recognized cause of severe HTG
items.content:
FPLD is a dominantly inherited (LMNA, PPARG, others) under-recognized monogenic cause of severe HTG. Adipose tissue is lost from the extremities and buttocks (giving a 'muscular' look) but excessive in visceral depots, producing both increased VLDL production and reduced LPL-mediated clearance.

The metabolic signature is severe HTG WITH severe insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, and hepatic steatosis, and ASCVD risk is substantially increased (unlike pure FCS). The diagnosis is clinical (fat-distribution + metabolic findings); a gene panel can confirm and — because it is dominant — should trigger family screening. Treat aggressively: statins (± additional LDL-lowering) for atherogenic lipoproteins plus aggressive management of the insulin-resistant diabetes. (Congenital GENERALIZED lipodystrophy is recessive, with leptin deficiency, treated with recombinant leptin.)
keyPoints: Dominant; lost limb/buttock fat, excess visceral fat ('muscular' look).
Severe HTG + severe insulin resistance/T2DM/fatty liver; ASCVD risk high.
Generalized lipodystrophy (recessive) → recombinant leptin.
items.reference: FPLD: "FPLD is an underrecognized monogenic cause of severe HTG... FPLD patients usually have insulin resistance, often quite severe, accompanied by type 2 diabetes and hepatosteatosis... ASCVD risk is substantially increased."

items.title: Why chylomicron remnant clearance depends on apoE, not apoB
items.content:
Chylomicrons carry apoB-48 (made in intestine by mRNA editing of the APOB gene), which LACKS the LDL-receptor-binding region present in hepatic apoB-100. So after LPL hydrolyzes their triglycerides, chylomicron REMNANTS cannot be cleared by the LDL receptor — they are removed by the liver via apoE as the ligand (apoE acquired from HDL).

This is why apoE matters so much: defective apoE (apoE2/E2 in FDBL) impairs remnant clearance and causes remnant accumulation, while the apoB-100 on VLDL/IDL/LDL allows those particles to be cleared by the LDL receptor. It also explains why FCS (LPL-pathway defects) leaves chylomicrons that can't even be lipolyzed.
keyPoints: Chylomicrons: apoB-48 (no LDL-receptor ligand) → cleared via apoE.
VLDL/IDL/LDL: apoB-100 → cleared by LDL receptor.
Defective apoE (E2/E2) → remnant accumulation (FDBL).
items.reference: VLDL/LDL transport: "Chylomicron remnants contain apoB-48, which lacks the region in apoB-100 that binds to the LDL receptor. Nevertheless, they are rapidly removed from the circulation by the liver through a process that critically requires apoE."

items.title: A practical workup of dyslipidemia
items.content:
Step 1: classify which lipoprotein classes are abnormal. Step 2: rule out SECONDARY causes — in elevated TG (>150) check fasting glucose/HbA1c (diabetes); in elevated LDL-C (>160) check TSH (hypothyroidism) and consider liver/kidney disease. Step 3: consider a primary genetic disorder, because the underlying defect informs pancreatitis risk (severe HTG), ASCVD risk, drug choice, and family screening.

Pattern recognition: TG >1000 with cholesterol:TG ratio strongly TG-predominant → FCS panel; severe HTG + central obesity/insulin resistance/fatty liver → FPLD; LDL-C >190 + family history → FH panel; LDL-C >190 with NORMAL-cholesterol parents → ARH/sitosterolemia/LALD; cholesterol ≈ TG with normal HDL + palmar xanthomas → FDBL (apoE2/E2); mixed dyslipidemia + high apoB + family history → FCHL.
keyPoints: Classify the pattern → exclude secondary causes → consider primary genetic disorder.
TG>150: check glucose; LDL>160: check TSH.
Use pattern cues (xanthomas, apoB, family history) to pinpoint the genetic disorder.
items.reference: Approach/Diagnosis: "In patients with elevated TG levels (>150 mg/dL), a fasting glucose and/or hemoglobin A1c should be obtained... In patients with elevated LDL-C levels (>160 mg/dL), a TSH should be obtained to rule out hypothyroidism."

items.title: Classic LCAT deficiency vs fish-eye disease
items.content:
LCAT esterifies free cholesterol within HDL (activated by apoA-I); its deficiency leaves a high proportion of free cholesterol in lipoproteins and impairs HDL maturation, giving very low HDL-C. Two forms:
• COMPLETE (classic) LCAT deficiency: corneal opacification PLUS hemolytic anemia and progressive renal insufficiency → end-stage renal disease.
• PARTIAL deficiency (FISH-EYE disease): corneal opacification only, no systemic disease.

Remarkably, despite very low HDL-C, premature ASCVD is NOT a consistent feature — reinforcing that low HDL-C is not necessarily atherogenic. Diagnose by assaying plasma LCAT activity or sequencing LCAT.
keyPoints: LCAT deficiency → ↑free cholesterol, very low HDL-C, corneal opacity.
Complete: + hemolytic anemia + renal failure; fish-eye: corneal only.
Premature ASCVD is not consistent (low HDL ≠ inevitable atherosclerosis).
items.reference: Familial LCAT Deficiency: "patients with complete LCAT deficiency have hemolytic anemia and progressive renal insufficiency... In partial LCAT deficiency, there are no other known clinical sequelae... premature ASCVD is not a consistent feature."

items.question: A fasting lipid panel shows total cholesterol 260, HDL-C 40, and triglycerides 700 mg/dL. How should LDL-C be determined?
options: Calculate it with the Friedewald formula
The Friedewald formula cannot be used (TG >400); measure LDL-C directly or use non-HDL-C
items.explanation: The Friedewald formula is invalid when TG >400 mg/dL because the TG/5 estimate of VLDL cholesterol becomes unreliable. LDL-C should be measured directly, or non-HDL-C (total − HDL = 220) used, which captures all atherogenic cholesterol.
items.reference: Screening: "by convention, it cannot be used if the TG level is >400 mg/dL. LDL-C can be directly measured by a number of methods."

items.question: A 28-year-old with fasting TG of 2200 mg/dL presents with acute pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, and lipemia retinalis; there is no premature heart disease in the family. What is the most likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Very high TG (often >1000), pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, and lipemia retinalis without premature ASCVD characterize familial chylomicronemia syndrome (impaired LPL-mediated lipolysis). FH causes high LDL-C; FDBL causes mixed elevation with palmar xanthomas; FCHL causes mixed dyslipidemia with high apoB.
items.reference: FCS: "FCS can present in childhood or adulthood with severe abdominal pain due to acute pancreatitis... Eruptive xanthomas... lipemia retinalis... Premature ASCVD is not generally a feature of FCS."

items.question: What is the cornerstone of management for familial chylomicronemia syndrome to prevent pancreatitis?
options: Marked dietary fat restriction (to ~15 g/day) with fat-soluble vitamin supplementation
items.explanation: Because the defect is impaired chylomicron lipolysis, treatment centers on severe dietary fat restriction (~15 g/day) with fat-soluble vitamins. Fibrates/fish oils are usually ineffective in FCS; statins/PCSK9 inhibitors target LDL-C (which is not the problem). APOC3/ANGPTL3 silencing are emerging options.
items.reference: FCS: "Dietary fat intake should be markedly restricted (to as little as 15 g/d), often with fat-soluble vitamin supplementation... fish oils or fibrates... are unlikely to be effective."

items.question: A 35-year-old with LDL-C 240 mg/dL, normal triglycerides, Achilles tendon xanthomas, and a father who had an MI at 45 most likely has which condition?
items.explanation: LDL-C >190 with normal TG, tendon xanthomas, and a family history of premature CHD is classic heterozygous FH (dominant; LDLR/APOB/PCSK9). It is a clinical diagnosis; cascade screening of first-degree relatives is indicated. Secondary causes (hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome) should be excluded.
items.reference: FH: "Heterozygous FH is characterized by elevated plasma levels of LDL-C (usually >190 mg/dL)... Physical findings... may include corneal arcus and/or tendon xanthomas, particularly involving the dorsum of the hands and the Achilles tendons."

items.question: A child with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia is receptor-negative (no LDL-receptor activity). Which therapy will be INEFFECTIVE?
items.explanation: Statins and PCSK9 inhibitors work by upregulating the LDL receptor, so they are ineffective in receptor-NEGATIVE HoFH (no functional receptor). Receptor-independent therapies — lomitapide, mipomersen, evinacumab — and LDL apheresis are used; receptor-defective patients may respond modestly to statins/PCSK9i.
items.reference: HoFH: "receptor-negative patients have no response to statins and PCSK9 inhibitors, receptor-defective patients can have modest responses."

items.question: A 55-year-old has total cholesterol 350 and triglycerides 360 mg/dL (elevated to a similar degree), normal HDL-C, orange-yellow discoloration of the palmar creases, and peripheral vascular disease. What genotype is diagnostic?
items.explanation: Similar elevations of cholesterol and TG, normal HDL, palmar xanthomas (xanthoma striata palmaris), and prominent PVD are characteristic of familial dysbetalipoproteinemia, caused by apoE2/E2 homozygosity (impaired remnant clearance). The Friedewald formula is invalid here; diagnose by VLDL-C:TG ratio >0.30 or apoE genotyping.
items.reference: FDBL: "the plasma levels of cholesterol and TG are often elevated to a similar degree, and the level of HDL-C is usually normal... DNA-based apoE genotyping can be performed to confirm homozygosity for apoE2."

items.question: A 48-year-old with TG 280, LDL-C 150, HDL-C 34 mg/dL, and a disproportionately HIGH apoB, with several relatives having early heart disease, most likely has:
items.explanation: Mixed dyslipidemia with low HDL, a strong family history of premature CHD, and an apoB disproportionately high relative to LDL-C (reflecting small dense LDL from hepatic VLDL/apoB overproduction) is familial combined hyperlipidemia — a common, highly atherogenic disorder requiring aggressive treatment.
items.reference: FCHL: "Patients with FCHL have substantially elevated plasma levels of apoB, often disproportionately high relative to the plasma LDL-C concentration, indicating the presence of small dense LDL particles."

items.question: A patient has an unexplained rise in LDL-C from 130 to 200 mg/dL. Which test should be done before diagnosing a primary lipid disorder?
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism is the most important medical cause of elevated LDL-C (it downregulates the hepatic LDL receptor), and it is often subtle. All patients with elevated LDL-C — especially an unexplained rise — should have TSH checked; thyroid replacement often normalizes LDL-C.
items.reference: Secondary Factors: "all patients presenting with elevated plasma levels of LDL-C, especially if there has been an unexplained increase in LDL-C, should be screened for hypothyroidism by measuring TSH."

items.question: A patient on atorvastatin 20 mg has LDL-C still above goal. Approximately how much additional LDL-C lowering will doubling the dose to 40 mg achieve?
items.explanation: Once on a statin, each doubling of the dose lowers LDL-C only about 6% further ('rule of 6'). Therefore, adding a complementary agent (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitor, bempedoic acid) is usually more effective than repeated dose-doubling.
items.reference: Statins: "once a patient is on a statin, the doubling of the statin dose produces a ~6% further reduction in the level of plasma LDL-C."

items.question: An asymptomatic patient on a statin has a routine creatine kinase that is mildly elevated. What is the appropriate response?
options: No action needed — routine CK monitoring is not recommended, and asymptomatic elevation does not predict myopathy
items.explanation: Serum CK need not be monitored routinely in statin users; an asymptomatic CK elevation does not predict myopathy and does not require stopping the drug. CK is measured when muscle SYMPTOMS occur, to distinguish myalgia from true myopathy.
items.reference: Statins: "Serum CK levels need not be monitored on a routine basis in patients taking statins because an elevated CK in the absence of symptoms does not predict the development of myopathy."

items.question: Which agent silences PCSK9 messenger RNA in the liver and is dosed subcutaneously every 6 months?
items.explanation: Inclisiran is a small interfering RNA (siRNA) targeting PCSK9 mRNA, lowering LDL-C ~60% with subcutaneous dosing every 6 months. Evolocumab/alirocumab are anti-PCSK9 antibodies dosed every 2 weeks. Ezetimibe (NPC1L1) and bempedoic acid (ATP citrate lyase) have other mechanisms.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "inclisiran, is a therapeutic siRNA molecule that targets the PCSK9 mRNA in the liver... it is administered subcutaneously every 6 months."

items.question: Why is bempedoic acid less likely to cause muscle symptoms than statins?
options: It is a prodrug activated by an enzyme (ACSVL1) not expressed in skeletal muscle, so the active drug is not generated in muscle
items.explanation: Bempedoic acid (an ATP citrate lyase inhibitor) is a prodrug requiring activation by ACSVL1, which is absent from skeletal muscle, so it does not form the active compound there — explaining less myalgia and making it useful in statin-intolerant patients. Watch for raised uric acid/gout; it does not increase diabetes.
items.reference: Bempedoic acid: "a prodrug that requires activation by very-long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase-1 (ASCVL1), which is not expressed in skeletal muscle, potentially explaining why it has less association with myalgias than statins."

items.question: Which triglyceride-lowering agent has been shown to REDUCE cardiovascular events in patients with hypertriglyceridemia?
items.explanation: Icosapent ethyl, an EPA-only omega-3 product, reduced cardiovascular events in patients with hypertriglyceridemia — a benefit not demonstrated for mixed EPA/DHA preparations. Bile acid sequestrants raise TG and are avoided in HTG.
items.reference: Omega-3 Fatty Acids: "Icosapent ethyl is an EPA-only product that has been shown to reduce cardiovascular events in patients with HTG."

items.question: By which mechanism do fibrates lower triglycerides?
options: Acting as PPARα agonists that stimulate LPL and reduce apoC-III
items.explanation: Fibrates are PPARα agonists that stimulate lipoprotein lipase activity, reduce apoC-III (enhancing remnant clearance), and promote fatty-acid β-oxidation, lowering TG ~30%. They can cause myopathy (especially with statins — avoid gemfibrozil combos), gallstones, and raised creatinine.
items.reference: Fibrates: "Fibric acid derivatives, or fibrates, are agonists of PPARα... Fibrates stimulate LPL activity... reduce apoC-III synthesis... promote β-oxidation of fatty acids."

items.question: Which statement about low HDL-C best reflects current understanding?
options: Raising HDL-C with medication is a proven way to reduce cardiovascular events
Low HDL-C is a useful risk marker but is not considered a direct causal target for therapy
Low HDL-C should always be treated with niacin
Low HDL-C has no association with cardiovascular risk
items.explanation: Low HDL-C is a strong independent risk marker (used in risk calculators) but is now considered NOT directly causal — drugs that raise HDL-C have not reduced events. Management focuses on the underlying drivers (TG, weight, insulin resistance) and lowering apoB/LDL-C, not on raising HDL-C.
items.reference: Low HDL: "it is now considered doubtful that low HDL-C is directly causal... it is no longer considered a target for therapeutic intervention to raise HDL-C levels."

items.question: A patient has HDL-C of 3 mg/dL, enlarged orange tonsils, hepatosplenomegaly, and peripheral neuropathy. Which gene is defective?
items.explanation: Extremely low HDL-C with enlarged orange tonsils, hepatosplenomegaly, and peripheral neuropathy is Tangier disease (ABCA1 deficiency). Without ABCA1, apoA-I cannot be lipidated and is rapidly cleared, so cholesterol accumulates in the reticuloendothelial system.
items.reference: Tangier Disease: "caused by mutations in the ABCA1 gene... extremely low circulating plasma levels of HDL-C (<5 mg/dL)... pathognomonic enlarged, grayish yellow or orange tonsils."

items.question: A child has severe hypercholesterolemia with tendon xanthomas, hemolysis, splenomegaly, and large platelets, and the LDL-C does NOT respond to statins. Which diagnosis and treatment fit best?
options: Sitosterolemia; ezetimibe and bile acid sequestrants (with dietary sterol restriction)
items.explanation: Severe hypercholesterolemia with hemolysis, splenomegaly, and megathrombocytes, plus poor statin response, suggests sitosterolemia (ABCG5/ABCG8 loss with plant-sterol hyperabsorption). Because the problem is absorption, ezetimibe, bile acid sequestrants, and a low-sterol diet — not statins — are most effective.
items.reference: Sitosterolemia: "Episodes of hemolysis and splenomegaly are a distinctive clinical feature... diet, bile acid sequestrants, and cholesterol-absorption inhibitors are the most effective agents."

items.question: A non-obese adult has elevated LDL-C, low HDL-C, and fatty liver without insulin resistance. A dried-blood-spot enzyme assay confirms the diagnosis. Which treatment is specific and highly effective?
items.explanation: This pattern suggests lysosomal acid lipase deficiency (LALD), diagnosed by a dried-blood-spot LAL activity assay. Enzyme replacement with sebelipase alfa is specific and highly effective. It is important to suspect LALD because it is treatable and otherwise progresses to hepatic fibrosis.
items.reference: LALD: "The diagnosis can be made with a dried blood spot assay of LAL activity... enzyme replacement therapy with sebelipase alfa is now available and is highly effective."

items.question: A child with fat malabsorption, areflexia, ataxia, pigmented retinopathy, and undetectable LDL-C and apoB has abetalipoproteinemia. What is the key treatment?
options: High-dose fat-soluble vitamin supplementation, especially vitamin E, with a low-fat diet
items.explanation: Abetalipoproteinemia (MTP loss) prevents secretion of apoB-containing particles, causing fat-soluble vitamin deficiency; the neurologic/retinal disease is driven by vitamin E deficiency. Treatment is a low-fat diet with high-dose vitamin E (plus A and K), started as early as possible.
items.reference: Abetalipoproteinemia: "Treatment consists of a low-fat, high-caloric, vitamin-enriched diet accompanied by large supplemental doses of vitamin E."

items.question: A 52-year-old man without ASCVD has a borderline 10-year risk and intermediate LDL-C. Which test best provides independent information to decide on a statin?
items.explanation: In borderline-risk primary-prevention patients (men >40, women >50), a coronary artery calcium score provides independent prognostic information: an elevated CAC supports starting a statin, while a CAC of zero can support deferral. ApoB, Lp(a), and hs-CRP are additional risk enhancers.
items.reference: Pharmacologic Therapy: "a coronary artery calcium (CAC) score has been shown to provide independent information about risk of future CAD... might be used to justify initiation of statin therapy in primary prevention."

items.question: In a patient with established ASCVD, to what LDL-C level is drug therapy supported by clinical trial data?
options: Stop once LDL-C is below 130 mg/dL
Continue lowering as long as LDL-C remains >70 mg/dL, using combination therapy if needed
items.explanation: In established ASCVD, trial data support lowering LDL-C with drug therapy as long as it remains above 70 mg/dL, adding combination therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitor, bempedoic acid) if needed. Patients with average LDL-C also benefit from further lowering.
items.reference: Hypercholesterolemia treatment: "In patients with established ASCVD, drug therapy to reduce LDL-C is well supported by clinical trial data to reduce LDL-C as long as it remains >70 mg/dL, using combination drug therapy if necessary."

items.question: In which patient should a bile acid sequestrant generally be AVOIDED?
options: A pregnant woman needing LDL-C lowering
items.explanation: Bile acid sequestrants can RAISE triglycerides (by driving hepatic VLDL output), so they are generally avoided in hypertriglyceridemia. Because they are not systemically absorbed, they are otherwise safe (including pregnancy/children).
items.reference: Bile Acid Sequestrants: "BAS... can cause an increase in plasma TGs. Therefore, patients with HTG generally should not be treated with bile acid-binding resins."

items.question: A patient with severe cholestatic liver disease has marked hypercholesterolemia and cutaneous xanthomas due to an abnormal particle that lacks apoB and is rich in free cholesterol. What is this particle?
items.explanation: Severe cholestasis sheds Lp-X, a lamellar particle lacking apoB with an aqueous core and rich in free cholesterol; it can deposit in skin to form xanthomas. Cholestasis raises LDL-C by downregulating the LDL receptor as cholesterol can't be excreted into bile.
items.reference: Liver Disorders: "excess free cholesterol, coupled with phospholipids, is shed into the plasma as a constituent of a lamellar particle called Lp-X. These unusual particles... lack apoB... are rich in free cholesterol, and can deposit in the skin, producing xanthomas."

items.question: A healthy asymptomatic adult is found to have an LDL-C of 25 mg/dL with low triglycerides. A loss-of-function mutation in which gene would explain this and confer protection from coronary disease?
items.explanation: Loss-of-function mutations in PCSK9 increase hepatic LDL-receptor activity and lower LDL-C, with substantial protection from coronary heart disease due to lifelong low LDL — the genetic insight that led to PCSK9-inhibitor drugs. LDLR loss-of-function would RAISE LDL-C.
items.reference: Familial PCSK9 Deficiency: "Heterozygosity for nonsense mutations in PCSK9... reduced plasma levels of LDL-C... substantial protection from CHD relative to those without a PCSK9 mutation."

items.question: A lean-limbed, 'muscular'-appearing woman has severe hypertriglyceridemia, severe insulin-resistant diabetes, and fatty liver, with loss of subcutaneous fat from the limbs and buttocks. What is the likely diagnosis?
items.explanation: Loss of subcutaneous fat from the extremities/buttocks with a 'muscular' appearance, severe HTG, severe insulin resistance/diabetes, and fatty liver characterizes familial partial lipodystrophy (dominant; LMNA/PPARG). Unlike FCS, ASCVD risk is substantially increased, so aggressive lipid and diabetes management is needed.
items.reference: FPLD: "FPLD is characterized by loss of subcutaneous fat in the extremities and buttocks... patients are often described as having a 'muscular' appearance... severe HTG, FPLD patients usually have insulin resistance... type 2 diabetes and hepatosteosis."

items.question: Why are chylomicron remnants cleared by the liver via apoE rather than the LDL receptor?
options: They contain apoB-48, which lacks the LDL-receptor-binding region
They are cleared by lipoprotein lipase
items.explanation: Chylomicrons carry apoB-48 (intestinal), which lacks the LDL-receptor-binding domain of apoB-100, so their remnants are cleared by the liver via apoE as the ligand. This is why defective apoE (apoE2/E2) causes remnant accumulation (FDBL).
items.reference: VLDL/LDL transport: "Chylomicron remnants contain apoB-48, which lacks the region in apoB-100 that binds to the LDL receptor... critically requires apoE as a ligand for receptors in the liver."

items.question: Ezetimibe lowers LDL-C by inhibiting which intestinal protein?
items.explanation: Ezetimibe binds and inhibits NPC1L1, the enterocyte transporter for cholesterol absorption, reducing sterol delivery to the liver and upregulating the LDL receptor (~18% LDL-C reduction, additive to statins and event-reducing).
items.reference: Ezetimibe: "Ezetimibe is a cholesterol absorption inhibitor that binds directly to and inhibits NPC1L1 and blocks the intestinal absorption of cholesterol."

items.question: In addition to managing precipitating conditions, what is first-line pharmacologic therapy for familial dysbetalipoproteinemia?
items.explanation: FDBL carries substantial ASCVD risk and should be treated aggressively. It is typically diet-responsive, and statins are first-line; ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, and fibrates are also effective. Precipitating conditions (diabetes, hypothyroidism, obesity, alcohol) should be addressed.
items.reference: FDBL: "Pharmacologic therapy is often required, and statins are the first line in management."

items.question: A patient with severe hypercholesterolemia and tendon xanthomas has parents with NORMAL cholesterol, and the hypercholesterolemia responds to statins. Which diagnosis is most consistent?
items.explanation: Severe hypercholesterolemia with normal-cholesterol parents suggests a recessive condition; autosomal recessive hypercholesterolemia (LDLRAP1) causes intermediate LDL elevation, tendon xanthomas, and — unlike homozygous FH — RESPONDS to statins (though often needs additional therapy). Receptor-negative HoFH does not respond to statins.
items.reference: ARH: "Unlike FH homozygotes, the hyperlipidemia responds to treatment with statins, but these patients often require additional therapy."

items.question: Which initial test is recommended in a patient found to have fasting triglycerides of 320 mg/dL?
options: Fasting glucose/HbA1c to rule out diabetes
items.explanation: In patients with elevated triglycerides (>150 mg/dL), a fasting glucose and/or HbA1c should be obtained to rule out diabetes, a major secondary cause. (For elevated LDL-C >160, TSH is checked to exclude hypothyroidism.) Secondary causes are excluded before pursuing a primary genetic disorder.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "In patients with elevated TG levels (>150 mg/dL), a fasting glucose and/or hemoglobin A1c should be obtained to rule out diabetes."

items.question: Evinacumab, approved for homozygous FH, works by inhibiting which target?
items.explanation: Evinacumab is a monoclonal antibody against ANGPTL3. ANGPTL3 inhibits LPL and endothelial lipase; blocking it lowers LDL-C (and TG) by a mechanism independent of the LDL receptor — valuable in receptor-negative HoFH where statins/PCSK9 inhibitors fail. The genetic basis came from ANGPTL3 loss-of-function (familial combined hypolipidemia).
items.reference: HoFH/ANGPTL3: "an antibody that inhibits ANGPTL3 are approved for the treatment of patients with HoFH... ANGPTL3... inhibits LPL... Deficiency of ANGPTL3, therefore, raises LPL activity and lowers blood TG; it also lowers LDL-C."

items.question: A transplant patient on cyclosporine and a patient with nephrotic syndrome both develop dyslipidemia. Which statement is correct?
options: Cyclosporine/sirolimus can raise LDL-C and TG, and nephrotic syndrome causes VLDL overproduction raising both TG and LDL-C
items.explanation: Immunosuppressants such as cyclosporine and sirolimus can substantially raise LDL-C and TG, and nephrotic syndrome is a classic cause of VLDL overproduction elevating both TG and LDL-C. Statins (with care for muscle effects/drug interactions) are often needed when the underlying cause persists.
items.reference: Secondary Factors: "cyclosporin and sirolimus, can cause substantial elevation in LDL-C and TG levels... Nephrotic syndrome is a classic cause of excessive VLDL production leading to elevation in both TGs and LDL-C."

items.statement: The Friedewald formula can be reliably used to calculate LDL-C when the triglyceride level is 600 mg/dL.
items.explanation: False. The Friedewald formula cannot be used when TG >400 mg/dL, because the TG/5 estimate of VLDL cholesterol becomes unreliable. LDL-C should be measured directly, or non-HDL-C used.
items.reference: Screening: "by convention, it cannot be used if the TG level is >400 mg/dL."

items.statement: Lipoprotein(a) is a causal, heritable, independent risk factor for ASCVD and aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: True. Lp(a) is established as a causal factor for ASCVD and aortic stenosis and is highly heritable; an elevated Lp(a) is an independent risk factor that warrants more aggressive LDL-C lowering.
items.reference: Screening: "Lp(a) is now established as causal factor for ASCVD and aortic stenosis, and an elevated level of Lp(a) serves as an independent risk factor."

items.statement: The principal danger of severe hypertriglyceridemia (TG >500 mg/dL) is acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: True. Severe HTG (chylomicronemia) is associated with acute pancreatitis, and the treatment goal is to lower TG below 500 to prevent it. Pure chylomicronemia is not strongly atherogenic; it is moderate HTG that raises ASCVD risk.
items.reference: Severe HTG: "it is associated with risk of acute pancreatitis... The goal is to prevent pancreatitis by reducing fasting TG levels to <500 mg/dL."

items.statement: Familial hypercholesterolemia is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.
items.explanation: False. FH is autosomal DOMINANT (LDLR, APOB, or gain-of-function PCSK9), so ~50% of a patient's first-degree relatives are affected — the basis for cascade screening. (Rare FH-like phenotypes such as ARH and sitosterolemia are recessive.)
items.reference: FH: "FH is an autosomal dominant disorder... ~50% of the patient's siblings and children can be expected to have FH."

items.statement: Receptor-negative homozygous FH patients respond well to statins and PCSK9 inhibitors.
items.explanation: False. Statins and PCSK9 inhibitors act by upregulating the LDL receptor, so receptor-negative HoFH patients have no response; receptor-independent therapies (lomitapide, mipomersen, evinacumab) and LDL apheresis are needed. Receptor-defective patients respond modestly.
items.reference: HoFH: "receptor-negative patients have no response to statins and PCSK9 inhibitors."

items.statement: Hypothyroidism should be excluded by measuring TSH in patients with elevated LDL-C.
items.explanation: True. Hypothyroidism is the most important medical cause of elevated LDL-C (downregulating the hepatic LDL receptor), and is often subtle, so TSH should be checked in patients with elevated LDL-C, especially an unexplained rise.
items.reference: Secondary Factors: "all patients presenting with elevated plasma levels of LDL-C... should be screened for hypothyroidism by measuring TSH."

items.statement: Routine periodic creatine kinase monitoring is recommended for all patients taking statins.
items.explanation: False. CK is not monitored routinely; an asymptomatic CK elevation does not predict myopathy or require stopping the drug. CK is checked when muscle symptoms develop, to distinguish myalgia from myopathy.
items.reference: Statins: "Serum CK levels need not be monitored on a routine basis in patients taking statins."

items.statement: Raising HDL-C with medication is an established strategy to reduce cardiovascular events.
items.explanation: False. Although low HDL-C is a risk marker, it is not considered directly causal, and drugs that raise HDL-C have not reduced cardiovascular events; raising HDL-C is no longer a therapeutic target.
items.reference: Low HDL: "it is no longer considered a target for therapeutic intervention to raise HDL-C levels in order to reduce cardiovascular risk."

items.statement: Bile acid sequestrants can raise triglyceride levels and should generally be avoided in hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: True. Bile acid sequestrants primarily lower LDL-C but can raise plasma triglycerides, so they are generally avoided in patients with hypertriglyceridemia.
items.reference: Bile Acid Sequestrants: "can cause an increase in plasma TGs. Therefore, patients with HTG generally should not be treated with bile acid-binding resins."

items.statement: Sitosterolemia responds well to high-intensity statin therapy.
items.explanation: False. Because sitosterolemia results from sterol HYPERABSORPTION (ABCG5/ABCG8 loss), it responds poorly to statins; diet, bile acid sequestrants, and cholesterol-absorption inhibitors (ezetimibe) are the most effective treatments.
items.reference: Sitosterolemia: "diet, bile acid sequestrants, and cholesterol-absorption inhibitors are the most effective agents to reduce LDL-C and plasma plant sterol levels."

items.statement: The neurologic and retinal complications of abetalipoproteinemia are largely due to fat-soluble vitamin (especially vitamin E) deficiency.
items.explanation: True. Because apoB-containing particles are absent, fat-soluble vitamins (especially vitamin E) cannot be transported, producing spinocerebellar degeneration and pigmented retinopathy; high-dose vitamin E is central to treatment.
items.reference: Abetalipoproteinemia: "Most of the clinical manifestations of abetalipoproteinemia result from defects in the absorption and transport of fat-soluble vitamins... markedly deficient in vitamin E."

items.statement: Icosapent ethyl (purified EPA) has been shown to reduce cardiovascular events in patients with hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: True. Icosapent ethyl, an EPA-only omega-3 product, reduced cardiovascular events in hypertriglyceridemic patients — a benefit not demonstrated for mixed EPA/DHA fish-oil preparations.
items.reference: Omega-3 Fatty Acids: "Icosapent ethyl is an EPA-only product that has been shown to reduce cardiovascular events in patients with HTG."

items.statement: Familial combined hyperlipidemia is characterized by a single fixed lipid phenotype that never changes.
items.explanation: False. FCHL phenotypes (high LDL-C, high TG, or both) can switch within the same individual over time depending on diet, weight, and insulin sensitivity; apoB is consistently and disproportionately elevated.
items.reference: FCHL: "The lipoprotein profile can switch among these three phenotypes in the same individual over time."

items.statement: Complete LCAT deficiency causes hemolytic anemia and progressive renal failure, whereas fish-eye disease (partial deficiency) causes corneal opacity without systemic disease.
items.explanation: True. Complete (classic) LCAT deficiency causes corneal opacification, hemolytic anemia, and progressive renal insufficiency; partial deficiency (fish-eye disease) causes corneal opacification only. Despite very low HDL, premature ASCVD is not a consistent feature.
items.reference: Familial LCAT Deficiency: "patients with complete LCAT deficiency have hemolytic anemia and progressive renal insufficiency... In partial LCAT deficiency, there are no other known clinical sequelae."

items.assertion: Patients with severe hypertriglyceridemia are treated to lower triglycerides below 500 mg/dL.
items.reason: Severe hypertriglyceridemia, particularly chylomicronemia, is associated with acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because severe HTG predisposes to acute pancreatitis, the goal of therapy is to reduce TG below 500 to prevent it.
items.reference: Severe HTG: "The goal is to prevent pancreatitis by reducing fasting TG levels to <500 mg/dL."

items.assertion: Family-based cascade screening is effective for identifying additional people with familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.reason: FH is an autosomal dominant disorder, so about half of a patient's first-degree relatives are affected.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Dominant inheritance means ~50% of first-degree relatives carry FH, making cascade screening of relatives a high-yield strategy.
items.reference: FH: "Inheritance of FH is dominant... ~50% of the patient's siblings and children can be expected to have FH. For this reason, family-based 'cascade screening' can be very effective."

items.assertion: Receptor-negative homozygous FH requires receptor-independent therapies such as lomitapide or evinacumab.
items.reason: Statins and PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL-C by upregulating the LDL receptor, which is absent in receptor-negative HoFH.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Since statins/PCSK9 inhibitors need a functional LDL receptor, receptor-negative HoFH needs therapies that act independently of the receptor (reducing apoB/VLDL production or ANGPTL3).
items.reference: HoFH: "receptor-negative patients have no response to statins and PCSK9 inhibitors... a small-molecule inhibitor of MTP and an antisense oligonucleotide to apoB, and an antibody that inhibits ANGPTL3 are approved."

items.assertion: The Friedewald formula should not be used to estimate LDL-C in familial dysbetalipoproteinemia.
items.reason: In FDBL the VLDL particles are depleted in triglyceride and enriched in cholesterol, violating the assumption behind the TG/5 term.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because FDBL VLDL is cholesterol-rich (not the normal 1:5 cholesterol:TG), the TG/5 estimate of VLDL cholesterol is invalid, so the Friedewald formula cannot be applied.
items.reference: FDBL: "The Friedewald formula for calculation of LDL-C is not valid in FDBL because the VLDL particles are depleted in TG and enriched in cholesterol."

items.reason: Thyroid hormone normally upregulates the hepatic LDL receptor, so its deficiency reduces LDL receptor activity.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because thyroid hormone increases LDL-receptor expression, hypothyroidism downregulates the receptor and reduces LDL clearance, raising LDL-C.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "It causes elevated plasma LDL-C levels due to downregulation of the hepatic LDL receptor, which is normally increased by the action of thyroid hormone."

items.assertion: Adding a second lipid-lowering agent is often preferable to repeatedly doubling the statin dose.
items.reason: Each doubling of a statin dose produces only about a 6% further reduction in LDL-C.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because of the 'rule of 6', dose-doubling yields diminishing returns, so combining with ezetimibe or a PCSK9 inhibitor achieves greater LDL-C lowering.
items.reference: Statins: "the doubling of the statin dose produces a ~6% further reduction in the level of plasma LDL-C."

items.assertion: Bempedoic acid is useful in patients with statin-associated muscle symptoms.
items.reason: It is a prodrug activated by an enzyme not expressed in skeletal muscle, so the active drug is not formed in muscle.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because bempedoic acid requires activation by ACSVL1 (absent from skeletal muscle), it generates little active drug in muscle, causing less myalgia and making it suitable for statin-intolerant patients.
items.reference: Bempedoic acid: "a prodrug that requires activation by very-long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase-1 (ASCVL1), which is not expressed in skeletal muscle, potentially explaining why it has less association with myalgias."

items.assertion: Low HDL-C is used in cardiovascular risk calculators despite not being a treatment target.
items.reason: HDL-C integrates other risk factors (it falls with high triglycerides, insulin resistance, obesity, and inflammation), making it a useful risk biomarker.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because HDL-C reflects multiple cardiovascular risk factors, it is a strong risk marker (used in calculators), even though raising it is not therapeutically beneficial.
items.reference: Low HDL: "the HDL-C measurement integrates a number of cardiovascular risk factors, potentially explaining its strong inverse association with ASCVD."

items.assertion: Bile acid sequestrants are safe to use in pregnancy.
items.reason: They are not systemically absorbed, so their side effects are limited to the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because bile acid sequestrants act in the gut lumen and are not absorbed, they are very safe systemically (usable in pregnancy/children), with side effects confined to the GI tract.
items.reference: Bile Acid Sequestrants: "Because BAS are not systemically absorbed, they are very safe... Side effects of resins are limited to the gastrointestinal tract."

items.assertion: Defective apoE (apoE2) causes accumulation of remnant lipoproteins.
items.reason: ApoE is the ligand required for hepatic clearance of chylomicron remnants and IDL, which lack the apoB-100 LDL-receptor-binding region.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because remnants depend on apoE (not apoB) for hepatic uptake, low-affinity apoE2 slows their clearance, causing remnant accumulation (familial dysbetalipoproteinemia).
items.reference: FDBL: "ApoE is present in multiple copies on chylomicron remnants and IDL and mediates their removal via hepatic lipoprotein receptors... chylomicron remnants and IDL containing apoE2 are removed from plasma at a slower rate."

items.reason: PCSK9 targets the LDL receptor for lysosomal degradation, so blocking it increases the number of LDL receptors available to clear LDL.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because PCSK9 normally promotes LDL-receptor degradation, antagonizing it (antibody or siRNA) preserves receptors and enhances LDL clearance, lowering LDL-C ~60%.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "Antibodies to PCSK9 sequester it and functionally increase the number of LDL receptors available to remove LDL from the blood."

items.assertion: Ezetimibe is an effective treatment for sitosterolemia.
items.reason: Sitosterolemia results from excessive intestinal absorption of sterols due to loss of ABCG5/ABCG8.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the defect is sterol hyperabsorption, a cholesterol-absorption inhibitor (ezetimibe) directly counteracts the mechanism — far more effective than statins, which target synthesis.
items.reference: Sitosterolemia: "cholesterol-absorption inhibitors are the most effective agents to reduce LDL-C and plasma plant sterol levels in these patients."
